title: Homework_6
text:
Question 1

##################################################
# FUNCTION: log_growth 
# Purpose: Creating a logistic growth model that returns a dataframe with columns for population size and time.
# Inputs: N0 = initial population size
# r = intrinsic growth rate 
# K = carrying capacity
# tfinal = ending time for simulation
# tstep  = time step increment
# Output: A dataframe with any time between 1:20 with intervals of 1
# Nt = population size at each time

##################################################

log_growth <- function(N0=100, r=0.3, K=150, tfinal=20, tstep=1) {
# time vector 
time <- seq(from=0, to=tfinal, by=tstep)
  
# logistic growth equation
Nt <- K / (1 + ((K - N0) / N0) * exp(-r * time))
  
# dataframe for plotting
df_log_growth <- data.frame(time=time, Nt=Nt)
  
return(df_log_growth)

}
log_growth()

   time       Nt
1     0 100.0000
2     1 109.4564
3     2 117.7019
4     3 124.6588
5     4 130.3671
6     5 134.9449
7     6 138.5490
8     7 141.3457
9     8 143.4914
10    9 145.1235
11   10 146.3567
12   11 147.2839
13   12 147.9783
14   13 148.4971
15   14 148.8837
16   15 149.1714
17   16 149.3853
18   17 149.5441
19   18 149.6620
20   19 149.7495
21   20 149.8143

# Question 2

##################################################
# Purpose: Take the data frame log_growth and create a plot of time as the x-axis and population as the y-axis
# Input: data frame with two columns of time and population
# Output: Plot

plot_log_growth <- function(df_log_growth) {

plot(x = df_log_growth$time, 
y = df_log_growth$Nt,
main = "Logistical Growth Model",
xlab = "Time in Years",
ylab = "Population Size",
type = "l")
}

df <- log_growth()
plot_log_growth(df)

title: Homework 6
text:
library(deSolve)
library(ggplot2)

############################################################
# FUNCTION: run_sir_model
# PURPOSE: Solve deterministic SIR model with deSolve
# INPUTS:
#   beta, gamma : transmission & recovery rates
#   N           : total population
#   S0, I0, R0  : initial conditions
#   t_start     : start time
#   t_end       : end time
#   dt          : timestep
# OUTPUT:
#   data.frame with columns: time, S, I, R
############################################################

run_sir_model <- function(
  beta    = 0.1,
  gamma   = 0.1,
  N       = 1000,
  S0      = 999,
  I0      = 1,
  R0      = 0,
  t_start = 0,
  t_end   = 160,
  dt      = 1
) {

  init  <- c(S = S0, I = I0, R = R0)
  times <- seq(t_start, t_end, by = dt)

  sir_equations <- function(time, state, parameters) {
    with(as.list(c(state, parameters)), {
      dS <- -beta * S * I / N
      dI <-  beta * S * I / N - gamma * I
      dR <-  gamma * I
      list(c(dS, dI, dR))
    })
  }

  out <- ode(
    y     = init,
    times = times,
    func  = sir_equations,
    parms = c(beta = beta, gamma = gamma, N = N)
  )

  as.data.frame(out)
}

# PURPOSE: Create a data frame with max infected, beta, and gamma with vectors of ОІ and Оі values ranging from 0 to 0.5 in steps of 0.01.
# INPUTS:
#   beta, gamma : transmission & recovery rates
#   N           : total population
#   S0, I0, R0  : initial conditions
#   t_start     : start time
#   t_end       : end time
#   dt          : timestep
# OUTPUT: df with columns, max_infected, beta, gamma

sir_para_sweep <- function(beta_vec, gamma_vec) {
  n_combos <- length(beta_vec) * length(gamma_vec)
  sir_result <- data.frame(
    max_infected = numeric(n_combos),
    beta = numeric(n_combos),
    gamma = numeric(n_combos)
  )
  
  counter <- 1
  for (b in beta_vec) {
    for (g in gamma_vec) {
      
      # uses previous and changes only beta and gamma 
      sir_out <- run_sir_model(beta = b, gamma = g)
      
      sir_result$max_infected[counter] <- max(sir_out$I)
      sir_result$beta[counter] <- b
      sir_result$gamma[counter] <- g
      counter <- counter + 1
    }
  }
    return(sir_result)
}


# FUNCTION: plot_sir_heatmap_ggplot
# PURPOSE: Create a heatmap of max(number of infected) with Transmission Rate, ОІ and Recovery Rate,Оі as your x and y axises
# INPUT: sweep_df : data frame with columns beta, gamma, max_infected
# OUTPUT: Heatmap

plot_sir_heatmap_ggplot <- function(sweep_df) {

  p <- ggplot(sweep_df, aes(x = beta, y = gamma, fill = max_infected)) +
    geom_tile() +
    scale_fill_gradient(
      low = "blue", 
      high = "red", 
      name = "Max Infected"
    ) +
    labs(
      x = expression("Transmission Rate (" * beta * ")"),
      y = expression("Recovery Rate (" * gamma * ")"),
      title = "SIR Model Parameter Sweep"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 20))
  
  return(p)
}

beta_vec  <- seq(0, 0.5, by = 0.01)
gamma_vec <- seq(0, 0.5, by = 0.01)
sweep_results <- sir_para_sweep(beta_vec, gamma_vec)
plot_sir_heatmap_ggplot(sweep_results)

library(tidyverse)

в”Ђв”Ђ Attaching core tidyverse packages в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ tidyverse 2.0.0 в”Ђв”Ђ
вњ” dplyr     1.1.4     вњ” readr     2.1.5
вњ” forcats   1.0.0     вњ” stringr   1.5.1
вњ” ggplot2   3.5.2     вњ” tibble    3.3.0
вњ” lubridate 1.9.4     вњ” tidyr     1.3.1
вњ” purrr     1.1.0     
в”Ђв”Ђ Conflicts в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ tidyverse_conflicts() в”Ђв”Ђ
вњ– dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
вњ– dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
в„№ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

#Observations on the data set variables

class(iris)

glimpse(iris)

Columns: 5

glimpse(iris2)

title: Untitled
text:
library("ggplot2")


# Created for loop to count zeros  

count_zero_loop <- function(vector) {
    counter <- 0
    for (z in seq_along(vector)) {
        if (vec[z] == 0) {
            counter <- counter + 1
        }
    }
    return(counter)
}

# Using subsetting instead of a loop

count_zero_subset <- function(vector) {
    return(sum(vector == 0))
}

# Function to create matrix in which each element is the product of the row number * the column number.

product_matrix <- function(rows, cols) {
    matrix(outer(1:rows, 1:cols, FUN = "*"), nrow = rows, ncol = cols)
}

# Simulate a dataset with 3 groups of data, each group drawn from a distribution with a different mean. The final data frame should have 1 column for group and 1 column for the response variable.

set.seed(1000)

three_group_dataset <- c(rep("Group_1", 10), rep("Group_2", 10), rep("Group_3", 10))

response <- c(rnorm(10, mean = 2, sd = 1), rnorm(10, mean = 7, sd = 1), rnorm(10, mean = 14, sd = 1))

df_three_group_dataset <- data.frame(group = three_group_dataset, res = response)

group_means <- tapply(df_three_group_dataset$res, df_three_group_dataset$group, mean) 
print(group_means)

  Group_1   Group_2   Group_3 
 1.670910  6.564419 14.309922 

# Write a custom function that 1) reshuffles the response variable, and 2) calculates the mean of each group in the reshuffled data. Store the means in a vector of length 3.

shuffle_values <- function(data) {
    data$res <- sample(data$res)
    group_means <- tapply(data$res, data$group, mean)
    return(group_means)
}

# Use a for loop to repeat the function in b 100 times. Store the results in a data frame that has 1 column indicating the replicate number and 1 column for each new group mean, for a total of 4 columns.

n_sim <- 100
sim <- data.frame(replicate = 1:n_sim, Group1_mean = rep(NA, n_sim), Group2_mean = rep(NA, n_sim), Group3_mean = rep(NA, n_sim))

for (z in seq_len(n_sim)) {
    means <- shuffle_values(df_three_group_dataset)
    sim$Group1_mean[z] <-means[1]
    sim$Group2_mean[z] <-means[2]
    sim$Group3_mean[z] <-means[3]
}

#Use qplot() to create a histogram of the means for each reshuffled group. Or, if you want a challenge, use ggplot() to overlay all 3 histograms in the same figure. How do the distributions of reshuffled means compare to the original means?

library(ggplot2)
group1_data <- data.frame(Mean = sim$Group1_mean)
group2_data <- data.frame(Mean = sim$Group2_mean)
group3_data <- data.frame(Mean = sim$Group3_mean)

#Histogram 1
ggplot(group1_data, aes(x = Mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "goldenrod", color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = group_means[1], color = "blue", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  labs(title = "Group 1 - Randomization Distribution",
       subtitle = paste("Original mean =", round(group_means[1], 2)),
       x = "Mean Value",
       y = "Count")

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
в„№ Please use `linewidth` instead.








#Histogram 2
ggplot(group2_data, aes(x = Mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "goldenrod", color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = group_means[2], color = "blue", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  labs(title = "Group 2 - Randomization Distribution",
       subtitle = paste("Original mean =", round(group_means[2], 2)),
       x = "Mean Value",
       y = "Count")







#Histogram 3
ggplot(group3_data, aes(x = Mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "goldenrod", color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = group_means[3], color = "blue", linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  labs(title = "Group 3 - Randomization Distribution",
       subtitle = paste("Original mean =", round(group_means[3], 2)),
       x = "Mean Value",
       y = "Count")







# the distributions reshuffled shows what the means would look like if there was no difference between difference groups. Since there were original group means above and belowed the reshuffled means, it shows that the differences are not due to chance but moreso that there are true differences between means.
